brief:
The most striking feature of this week's curve is not what moved but what didn't — every single tenor printed flat, zero basis points of change across the entire term structure, from the 91-day bill at **9.85%** through the 15-year bond at **12.68%**. In a market where CPI is running at **9.13%**, where revenue collection has reached only **44.9%** of the FY26 target, and where the government's borrowing needs for the final quarter are about to intensify dramatically, a completely frozen curve is not a sign of equilibrium — it is a sign of a market holding its breath. The short end is well-anchored: the 91-day bill at **9.85%** sits just **15 basis points** below the repo rate of **10%**, and the call money rate at **9.8%** confirms that overnight liquidity is comfortable. But look at the shape beyond that and something unusual emerges. The 364-day bill at **10.07%** is actually **13 basis points below** the 182-day at **10.2%**, creating a clear inversion in the belly of the T-bill curve. Meanwhile, the 2-year bond at **11.95%** trades **80 basis points above** the 5-year at **10.82%** and **113 basis points above** the 10-year at **11.15%**, producing a grotesque kink that tells you the market is pricing severe near-term supply anxiety in the 2-year bucket while treating the longer end as relatively safe haven. The 15-year at **12.68%** sits a full **103 basis points above** the 20-year at **11.65%**, another distortion that screams illiquidity premium rather than fundamental term structure logic.

The structural picture explains these distortions. Excess liquidity at **৳1.9 lakh crore** is abundant by any historical standard, yet this abundance has not translated into aggressive bidding at auctions — the 10-year BGTB on March 8 cleared at a bid-cover of only **1.4x** with **10% devolvement** on Bangladesh Bank, the only auction in the recent set that failed to clear voluntarily. Banks are flush with deposits (ADR at a modest **75.8%**) and liquid reserves, but they are choosing to park money short rather than extend duration. The reason is threefold: first, with food inflation at **9.3%** and headline CPI stubbornly above **9%**, there is no conviction that the next policy move is a cut — if anything, the corridor ceiling at **11.5%** reminds everyone where rates could go. Second, the fiscal arithmetic is deteriorating; with ADP implementation at a dismal **11.5%** and revenue running nearly **55%** below target, the government will inevitably front-load borrowing in the April-June quarter to finance the deficit, and every treasurer in Dhaka knows that means a wall of supply is coming. Third, with total public debt at **৳21.5 lakh crore** — roughly **40% of GDP** — and interest payments consuming **22%** of revenue, the sovereign credit story is not improving, and the 15-year's **12.68%** yield is the market's way of demanding compensation for that trajectory. M2 growth at **9.5%** is running below nominal GDP growth, which means real monetary conditions are actually tightening even as headline liquidity looks plentiful — a subtle but important divergence that supports maintaining a cautious duration posture.

My recommendation to this committee is clear: stay short and opportunistic. The 91-day and 364-day bills at **9.85%** and **10.07%** respectively offer positive real carry against the call money rate, with clean bid-covers of **1.92x** and **1.75x** and zero devolvement — this is where the market has genuine two-way appetite, and we should be adding to our SLR portfolio here. The 5-year at **10.82%** with a **1.6x** bid-cover and no devolvement is the cleanest point on the bond curve and represents the best risk-adjusted entry if you want some duration — I would selectively accumulate here on any backup toward **11%**. Avoid the 2-year entirely; its **11.95%** yield looks generous but that kink exists because nobody wants to be stuck holding paper that will reprice violently if BB tightens, and the liquidity in that bucket is thin. The 10-year's **10% devolvement** is an early warning signal — if the next 10-year auction devolves again or the bid-cover drops below **1.3x**, we should interpret that as the market telling us the fiscal borrowing program is becoming unsustainable at current yields, which means a repricing higher is coming. The single most important catalyst to watch is the April revenue data: if collections do not accelerate sharply toward target, the government will be forced into aggressive Q4 borrowing that could push the 5-to-10-year segment **30-50 basis points** wider. In that scenario, patience will have been the most profitable trade we could have made.
